title: Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man

infobox.value: Catalog of all known human genes and genetic phenotypes.
[Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine")

(**OMIM**) is a continuously updated catalog of [human genes](/wiki/Human_genome "Human genome") and [genetic disorders](/wiki/Genetic_disorder "Genetic disorder") and [traits](/wiki/Phenotypic_trait "Phenotypic trait"), with a particular focus on the [gene-phenotype relationship](/wiki/Genotype-phenotype_distinction "Genotype-phenotype distinction"). As of 28 June 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Online_Mendelian_Inheritance_in_Man&action=edit), approximately 9,000 of the over 25,000 entries in OMIM represented [phenotypes](/wiki/Phenotype "Phenotype"); the rest represented [genes](/wiki/Gene "Gene"), many of which were related to known phenotypes.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

["OMIM Entry Statistics"](http://www.omim.org/statistics/entry). *Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man*. Baltimore, MD: McKusick-Nathans Institute of Genetic Medicine, Johns Hopkins University. 2019. Retrieved 28 June 2019.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Online catalog of human genes, with a particular focus on the gene-phenotype relationship

Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man

| Content | |
| --- | --- |
| Description | Catalog of all known human genes and genetic phenotypes. |
| Data types captured | [Genes](/wiki/Human_genome "Human genome"), [genetic disorders](/wiki/Genetic_disorder "Genetic disorder"), [phenotypic traits](/wiki/Phenotypic_trait "Phenotypic trait") |
| [Organisms](/wiki/Organism "Organism") | *[Homo sapiens](/wiki/Homo_sapiens "Homo sapiens")* |
| Contact | |
| [Research center](/wiki/Research_center "Research center") | [Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine") |
| Primary citation | [PMID](/wiki/PMID_(identifier) "PMID (identifier)") [21472891](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21472891) |
| Access | |
| Website | <http://www.omim.org/> |

**Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man** (**OMIM**) is a continuously updated catalog of [human genes](/wiki/Human_genome "Human genome") and [genetic disorders](/wiki/Genetic_disorder "Genetic disorder") and [traits](/wiki/Phenotypic_trait "Phenotypic trait"), with a particular focus on the [gene-phenotype relationship](/wiki/Genotype-phenotype_distinction "Genotype-phenotype distinction"). As of 28 June 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Online_Mendelian_Inheritance_in_Man&action=edit), approximately 9,000 of the over 25,000 entries in OMIM represented [phenotypes](/wiki/Phenotype "Phenotype"); the rest represented [genes](/wiki/Gene "Gene"), many of which were related to known phenotypes.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Versions and history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Online_Mendelian_Inheritance_in_Man&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Versions and history")]

OMIM is the online continuation of [Victor A. McKusick](/wiki/Victor_A._McKusick "Victor A. McKusick")'s *Mendelian Inheritance in Man* (MIM), which was published in 12 editions between 1966 and 1998.[[2]](#cite_note-MIM1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Nearly all of the 1,486 entries in the first edition of MIM discussed phenotypes.[[2]](#cite_note-MIM1-2)

MIM/OMIM is produced and curated at the [Johns Hopkins School of Medicine](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins School of Medicine") (JHUSOM). OMIM became available on the internet in 1987 under the direction of the [Welch Medical Library](/w/index.php?title=Welch_Medical_Library&action=edit&redlink=1 "Welch Medical Library (page does not exist)") at JHUSOM with financial support from the [Howard Hughes Medical Institute](/wiki/Howard_Hughes_Medical_Institute "Howard Hughes Medical Institute"). From 1995 to 2010, OMIM was available on the [World Wide Web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web") with [informatics](/wiki/Informatics "Informatics") and financial support from the [National Center for Biotechnology Information](/wiki/National_Center_for_Biotechnology_Information "National Center for Biotechnology Information"). The current OMIM website ([OMIM.org](http://www.omim.org)), which was developed with funding from JHUSOM, is maintained by [Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University"), with financial support from the [National Human Genome Research Institute](/wiki/National_Human_Genome_Research_Institute "National Human Genome Research Institute").[[5]](#cite_note-new_face-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Collection process and use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Online_Mendelian_Inheritance_in_Man&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Collection process and use")]

The content of MIM/OMIM is based on selection and review of the published [peer-reviewed](/wiki/Scholarly_peer_review "Scholarly peer review") biomedical literature. Updating of content is performed by a team of science writers and curators under the direction of [Ada Hamosh](/wiki/Ada_Hamosh "Ada Hamosh") at the McKusick-Nathans Institute of Genetic Medicine of Johns Hopkins University. While OMIM is freely available to the public, it is designed for use primarily by physicians and other health care professionals concerned with genetic disorders, by genetics researchers, and by advanced students in science and medicine.[[5]](#cite_note-new_face-5)

The database may be used as a resource for locating literature relevant to inherited conditions,[[7]](#cite_note-7) and its [numbering system](#MIM_classification_system) is widely used in the medical literature to provide a unified index for genetic diseases.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## OMIM classification system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Online_Mendelian_Inheritance_in_Man&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: OMIM classification system")]

See also: [List of OMIM disorder codes](/wiki/List_of_OMIM_disorder_codes "List of OMIM disorder codes")

### OMIM numbers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Online_Mendelian_Inheritance_in_Man&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: OMIM numbers")]

Each OMIM entry is given a unique six-digit identifier[[9]](#cite_note-FAQ1.2-9) as summarized below:

* 100000–299999: Autosomal [loci](/wiki/Locus_(genetics) "Locus (genetics)") or phenotypes (entries created before May 15, 1994)
* 300000–399999: [X-linked](/wiki/X-linked "X-linked") loci or phenotypes
* 400000–499999: [Y-linked](/wiki/Y-linked "Y-linked") loci or phenotypes
* 500000–599999: [Mitochondrial](/wiki/Mitochondrial "Mitochondrial") loci or phenotypes
* 600000 and above: [Autosomal](/wiki/Autosomal "Autosomal") loci or phenotypes (entries created after May 15, 1994)

In cases of [allelic heterogeneity](/wiki/Allelic_heterogeneity "Allelic heterogeneity"), the MIM number of the entry is followed by a decimal point and a unique 4-digit number specifying the variant.[[9]](#cite_note-FAQ1.2-9) For example, allelic variants in the [HBB](/wiki/HBB "HBB") gene (141900) are numbered 141900.0001 through 141900.0538.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Because OMIM has responsibility for the classification and naming of genetic disorders, these numbers are stable identifiers of the disorders.[[5]](#cite_note-new_face-5)

### Symbols preceding MIM numbers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Online_Mendelian_Inheritance_in_Man&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Symbols preceding MIM numbers")]

Symbols preceding MIM numbers[[11]](#cite_note-FAQ1.3-11) indicate the entry category:

* An asterisk (\*) before an entry number indicates a gene.
* A number symbol (#) before an entry number indicates that it is a descriptive entry, usually of a phenotype, and does not represent a unique locus. The reason for the use of the number symbol is given in the first paragraph of the entry. Discussion of any gene(s) related to the phenotype resides in another entry (or entries) as described in the first paragraph.
* A plus sign (+) before an entry number indicates that the entry contains the description of a gene of known sequence and a phenotype.
* A percent sign (%) before an entry number indicates that the entry describes a confirmed Mendelian phenotype or phenotypic locus for which the underlying molecular basis is not known.
* No symbol before an entry number generally indicates a description of a phenotype for which the Mendelian basis, although suspected, has not been clearly established or that the separateness of this phenotype from that in another entry is unclear.
* A [caret](/wiki/Caret "Caret") (^) before an entry number means the entry no longer exists because it was removed from the database or moved to another entry as indicated.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Online_Mendelian_Inheritance_in_Man&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Mendelian inheritance](/wiki/Mendelian_inheritance "Mendelian inheritance")
* [Online Mendelian Inheritance in Animals](/wiki/Online_Mendelian_Inheritance_in_Animals "Online Mendelian Inheritance in Animals")
* [Medical classification](/wiki/Medical_classification "Medical classification")
* [Comparative Toxicogenomics Database](/wiki/Comparative_Toxicogenomics_Database "Comparative Toxicogenomics Database"), a database that integrates chemicals and genes with human diseases, including OMIM data.
* [DECIPHER](/wiki/DECIPHER "DECIPHER"), a database of chromosomal imbalance and associated phenotype in humans, using [Ensembl](/wiki/Ensembl "Ensembl") resources.
* [MARRVEL](/w/index.php?title=MARRVEL&action=edit&redlink=1 "MARRVEL (page does not exist)"), a website that uses OMIM as one of the six human genetic databases and seven model organism databases to integrate information.
